The Langhe

11 Communes · 181 MGAs · ~2,100 ha under vine
The Barolo Zone
🗺 Detailed Polaner Map (PDF) ↗
🍇What is Barolo?
Barolo is a DOCG wine produced exclusively from Nebbiolo grapes in the Langhe hills of southern Piedmont. Often called the "King of Wines," it requires a minimum of 38 months aging (18 in wood) before release — 62 months for Riserva. The production zone spans 11 communes across three parallel ridges, divided by the Talloria dell'Annunziata valley into a western and eastern bank with distinctly different soils, climates, and wine styles.
~2,100 ha
Total vineyard
181
Official MGAs
38 months
Min. aging
11
Communes
🪨The Two Soils — West to East
Barolo's terroir divides along a geological fault. Understanding this split is the key to reading any label.
The 11 Communes
CF
Castiglione Falletto
153 ha · 20 MGAs · The "heart" — dual-soil balance of power & elegance
›
LM
La Morra
~550 ha · 39 MGAs · Largest commune — perfumed, elegant, approachable
›
BA
Barolo
~175 ha · 24 MGAs · Home of Cannubi — aromatic finesse, classic balance
›
SA
Serralunga d'Alba
~310 ha · 34 MGAs · Most powerful & long-lived — iron structure, austere
›
MA
Monforte d'Alba
~310 ha · 32 MGAs · Rich & muscular — home of Bussia & Ginestra
›
Other Communes
VE
Verduno
~45 ha · 8 MGAs · Smallest — delicate, Monvigliero, Pelaverga country
›
NO
Novello
~78 ha · 10 MGAs · Cool altitude — home of Ravera, Elvio Cogno
›
Minor Communes
GC
Grinzane Cavour
~40 ha · 5 MGAs · Historic castle, limited production
DA
Diano d'Alba
~15 ha · 3 MGAs · Better known for Dolcetto than Barolo
RO
Roddi
~25 ha · 3 MGAs · Small but rising — watch for value
CH
Cherasco
~15 ha · 3 MGAs · Northernmost fringe, rare bottlings

Castiglione Falletto

The Heart of Barolo — where power meets elegance
153
Hectares
20
MGAs
210–370m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Castiglione Falletto
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
Castiglione Falletto sits on the central ridge of the Barolo zone, bordering La Morra to the west and Serralunga to the east. It is one of only three communes lying entirely within the appellation.
Unique dual-soil character: The commune straddles the geological fault between younger Tortonian Sant'Agata Fossili Marls (blue-grey calcareous clay) and older Serravallian Diano Sandstone (compacted sandy-limestone). Some vineyards sit on both within a single parcel. This is why Castiglione's best wines achieve both the perfume of La Morra and the backbone of Serralunga — the "multi-site balance" that Bartolo Mascarello idealized in his commune blends.
Sant'Agata Marls (west side)
Diano Sandstone (east side)
Top Crus
Rocche di Castiglione ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
16.33 ha
Elevation
300–350 m
Exposure
Southeast, with east and south sections
Soil
Sandy Sant'Agata Fossili Marls overlying Diano Sandstone. The Lipulot sub-area is pure Diano Sandstone — dazzling white marl becoming sandier southward.
Style
Perfumed, refined, sensual — graceful without lacking strength. One of the steepest sites in all Barolo. Historically the most aromatic wines of Castiglione.
Producers
Vietti Brovia Oddero Roagna F.lli Monchiero
Collector note: Vietti's 1961 Rocche was among the first single-vineyard Barolos ever bottled. The consensus top cru of Castiglione — rated first-tier by Ratti, Masnaghetti, and Galloni. Worth buying in every strong vintage.
Monprivato ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
~7 ha (Mascarello owns ~6.13 ha — near-monopole)
Elevation
240–320 m (core ~280 m)
Exposure
Southwest
Soil
Silty, clay blue-grey marl packed with active limestone (Sant'Agata Fossili Marls of marine Miocene origin). Often compared to Burgundy's Côte d'Or.
Style
Among Barolo's most aromatic and "truffliest" — intense but understated structure, fine tannins, great longevity. The rare Michét clone adds depth. The Riserva Cà d'Morissio (~4,000 bottles, top years only) is one of the Langhe's most sought-after wines.
Producers
Giuseppe Mascarello Giovanni Sordo
Collector note: Historic since land registries of 1666. First separate bottling in 1970 at Luigi Veronelli's urging. Listed by Renato Ratti among Barolo's ~11 greatest historic vineyards. The Riserva Cà d'Morissio sits alongside Monfortino and Giacosa's red labels in the Barolo pantheon. Prioritize 2016, 2013, 2010.
Villero ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
22.07 ha (17+ owners)
Elevation
230–350 m
Exposure
Southwest
Soil
Similar marls to Monprivato but more clay-rich and structured. Masculine terroir — bold, muscular wines.
Style
Plush fruit, formidable character, slow-evolving. Often austere when young — needs 10–15+ years. The most powerful cru of Castiglione.
Producers
Vietti G. Mascarello Brovia Oddero G. Fenocchio Livia Fontana Sordo
Collector note: Vietti's Riserva Villero (made ~once every 4 years) earned perfect 100-point scores from The Wine Advocate in both 2007 and 2009 vintages, and 99 in 2010. This is the cru for maximum structure and cellaring ambition.
Bricco Boschis ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
~17.6 ha (near-monopole: Cavallotto)
Elevation
~230–330 m
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Center sits exactly on the Serravallian/Tortonian boundary — an extraordinary mix of white, yellow and grey marls punctuated by sand veins.
Style
Perfumed and finessed yet with substance and structure for long aging. Not forbiddingly tannic. Transparent terroir expression.
Producers
Cavallotto
Collector note: Estate founded 1928, ~23 ha contiguous. Staunch traditionalist — long maceration, neutral large cask. The Vigna San Giuseppe Riserva from the cru's sweet spot is one of Barolo's most under-appreciated trophy bottlings. Excellent value relative to peers.
Fiasco ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
~8 ha
Elevation
225–285 m
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Predominantly sandy Sant'Agata Fossili Marls (locally "töv"/tufa) with some sandstone and clay. Across the valley from Cannubi.
Style
More aromatic elegance than neighbouring Villero — floral, silky-tannined, bright. Accessible relatively young but ages gracefully.
Producers
Paolo Scavino Azelia
Collector note: Two cousins share the hill. Scavino's Bric dël Fiasc (first 1978) is the estate's signature. Azelia's Bricco Fiasco comes from ~85-year-old vines on white calcareous soil — a hidden gem.
Bricco Rocche ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
1.46 ha — smallest MGA in all of Barolo
Elevation
350–370 m (highest in Castiglione)
Exposure
Southeast to southwest
Soil
Diano Sandstone with silt and clay. Nestled between Villero and Rocche di Castiglione.
Style
A "microcosm of Barolo" — ethereal, elegant, pale, perfumed. Harmonises power, perfume, structure and finesse.
Producers
Ceretto (monopole since mid-1970s)
Collector note: Once among Italy's most expensive wines. Exclusive monopole — only available from Ceretto. A curio for completists and lovers of altitude-driven elegance.
Other Notable MGAs
Pira Notable ›
Area
~7 ha (Roagna monopole since 1989)
Elevation
300–350 m
Exposure
Southeast / east
Soil
Multiple strata of white limestone, grey and blue marl, and sand with high iron — derived from the breakdown of the Rocche above.
Style
Super-traditional, long-macerated, structured, mineral, age-worthy. Luca Roagna's Pira Vecchie Viti is a cult wine.
Producers
Roagna
Scarrone Notable ›
Area
~20 ha
Elevation
~250–320 m
Exposure
Varied (N through SE to SW — wraps around the town)
Soil
Clayey-calcareous grey-blue marl.
Producers
Luigi Oddero Fratelli Giacosa
Parussi Notable ›
Area
~13 ha
Elevation
220–290 m
Exposure
Southwest / west
Style
Northernmost notable cru; ripe and fruity with velvety tannins.
Producers
Massolino Bovio
Mariondino (Monriondino) Notable ›
Exposure
West/northwest (cooler portion)
Style
Shares Villero's amphitheater position.
Producers
Parusso Cascina Fontana
Altenasso (Garblet Suè) Notable ›
A.k.a.
Garblet Suè / Garbelletto Superiore
Producers
Brovia (bottled as "Garblet Suè")
Remaining MGAs (8) ›
Brunella · Codana (9.66 ha) · Montanello (borders Bricco Boschis, higher clay) · Pernanno · Piantà (Plantà) · Pugnane · Solanotto · Valentino · Vignolo

These are smaller and/or lower-profile MGAs on typical Sant'Agata Fossili Marls. Less frequently bottled as single-cru wines — historically used in commune blends.
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Long maceration · large Slavonian / old oak botti
Cavallotto Giuseppe Mascarello Roagna Cascina Fontana
Terroir-First / In-Between
Rejecting the dichotomy · quality- and site-focused
Vietti Brovia Azelia Oddero
Modernist (historically)
New barriques (now pulling back) · shorter maceration
Paolo Scavino Ceretto
Note: The "Barolo Wars" distinction is increasingly outdated. Scavino has substantially pulled back from barriques, and most producers now sit on a spectrum. Enrico Scavino, a pivotal figure in modern Barolo alongside Elio Altare, passed away in early 2024; daughters Enrica and Elisa now lead the estate. Vietti was acquired by the Krause family in 2016.
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
G. Mascarello — Monprivato Riserva Cà d'Morissio
~4,000 bottles, top years only. Michét clone. Alongside Monfortino in the Barolo pantheon.
🍷
Vietti — Riserva Villero
Made ~once per 4 years. 100 pts WA in 2007 & 2009 vintages.
🍷
Vietti — Rocche di Castiglione
Pioneer single-vineyard bottling since 1961.
🍷
Cavallotto — Bricco Boschis Vigna San Giuseppe Riserva
Under-appreciated trophy. Traditional winemaking, geological sweet spot.
🍷
Brovia — Rocche di Castiglione / Villero / Garblet Suè
Three Castiglione crus from one meticulous producer. Terroir transparency.
🍷
Roagna — Pira Vecchie Viti
Cult wine. Super-traditional, age-worthy, mineral-driven.
🍷
Scavino — Bric dël Fiasc
Estate signature since 1978. Once the modernist flagship, now more balanced.
🍷
Azelia — Bricco Fiasco
~85-year-old vines on white calcareous soil. Hidden gem — floral, silky.
📋Collector Strategy
For perfume & finesse: Rocche di Castiglione, Bricco Rocche, Monprivato
For power & long holding: Villero, Bricco Boschis, Pira
For value & terroir transparency: Brovia's three crus, Azelia & Scavino Fiasco
Everyday cellaring: Buy Vietti Barolo "Castiglione" and Brovia's classico every vintage but the worst; hold 1–2 years. Then pair with one trophy vertical (Mascarello Monprivato, Cavallotto San Giuseppe, or Vietti Rocche) held as long as possible.

La Morra

The Largest Commune — perfume, silk, and seductive Nebbiolo
~550
Hectares
39
MGAs
200–400m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — La Morra
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
The largest commune in the Barolo zone, accounting for roughly a quarter of total production. La Morra occupies the western ridge, with vineyards fanning out in every direction from the hilltop village — offering panoramic views of the entire appellation.
Tortonian heartland: Predominantly Sant'Agata Fossili Marls — younger blue-grey calcareous clay that produces Barolo's most perfumed, silken, and approachable wines. Red cherry, rose, anise. Higher sand content in some sites adds aromatic lift. The steep southeast-facing "Rocche" ridge (Brunate, Cerequio, La Serra, Fossati) adds mineral austerity to the elegance — these are La Morra's "Champions League" sites.
Sant'Agata Marls (dominant)
Top Crus
Brunate (shared with Barolo) ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Sant'Agata Marls with some sand. On the steep Rocche ridge, shared between La Morra and Barolo commune.
Style
One of Barolo's most celebrated crus. Marries La Morra's seductive perfume with mineral austerity — extraordinary complexity and longevity. Floral yet structured.
Producers
Roberto Voerzio Ceretto Vietti Marcarini Francesco Rinaldi
Cerequio ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Adjacent to Brunate on the Rocche ridge — equally prestigious. Elegant, intense, with remarkable purity. Slightly more refined than Brunate.
Producers
Roberto Voerzio Michele Chiarlo
Rocche dell'Annunziata ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South to southeast
Style
A great amphitheater site — one of La Morra's most esteemed. Wines of depth and elegance with fine-grained tannins. Beautiful mid-palate richness.
Producers
Renato Ratti Aurelio Settimo Trediberri Bartolo Mascarello (source)
Collector note: Renato Ratti — the cartographer who first mapped Barolo's crus in 1976 — was based here. His Barolo Rocche dell'Annunziata Marcenasco remains a benchmark.
La Serra ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South
Style
On the steep Rocche ridge alongside Brunate and Cerequio. Perfumed and elegant with a mineral edge. One of the highest-regarded La Morra crus.
Producers
Marcarini Roberto Voerzio
Arborina ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
Southeast
Style
Warm, ripe, generous — more accessible young than the Rocche ridge crus. Lush fruit with supple tannins. Very popular site.
Producers
Elio Altare Mauro Veglio Corino
Collector note: Elio Altare — the father of modern Barolo — is based here. His wines from Arborina helped launch the modernist revolution in the 1980s.
Other Notable MGAs
Conca · Gattera · Torriglione · Capalot Notable›
Conca — South-facing amphitheater; Renato Ratti's second cru
Gattera — East of Annunziata; rising reputation
Torriglione — Near Annunziata; full-bodied La Morra
Capalot — Central position; Mauro Veglio
Boiolo · Serradenari · Roggeri · Silio Notable›
Boiolo — South of the village; soft, elegant
Serradenari — Higher altitude; cooler, more structured
Roggeri — Central; Mauro Molino
Silio — North of Annunziata
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Fratelli Revello Aurelio Settimo
Terroir-First / In-Between
Roberto Voerzio Renato Ratti Marcarini Mauro Veglio Mauro Molino Trediberri Cordero di Montezemolo
Modernist
Elio Altare
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
Roberto Voerzio — Brunate / Cerequio / La Serra
Low yields, extraordinary concentration. Among Barolo's most expensive wines.
🍷
Elio Altare — Arborina
The modernist benchmark. Transformed Barolo in the 1980s.
🍷
Renato Ratti — Rocche dell'Annunziata Marcenasco
The cartographer's own wine. Classic, elegant, balanced.
🍷
Marcarini — Brunate / La Serra
Excellent value relative to prestige. Traditional La Morra elegance.
🍷
Trediberri — Rocche dell'Annunziata
Rising star — precise, transparent, great value.
📋Collector Strategy
For perfume & elegance: Brunate, Cerequio, La Serra (the Rocche ridge)
For approachable richness: Arborina, Rocche dell'Annunziata
For value: Trediberri, Mauro Molino, Marcarini — stunning quality at La Morra prices
Key principle: La Morra wines are the most approachable young, but the top crus (Brunate, Cerequio) rival any commune for aging. Drink warmer vintages (2017, 2020) earlier; cellar 2016/2019/2021 long.

Barolo

The Namesake — home of Cannubi, the original heartland
~175
Hectares
24
MGAs
220–380m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Barolo
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
The commune that gave its name to the wine sits at the physical and metaphorical heart of the zone. One of only three communes lying entirely within the appellation (with Castiglione Falletto and Serralunga). Smaller than its neighbours but punching far above its weight in prestige — largely thanks to Cannubi.
Mixed soil character: Primarily Tortonian Sant'Agata Fossili Marls on the western side (toward La Morra), transitioning to some Serravallian influence to the east (toward Monforte/Castiglione). The hill of Cannubi itself is geologically complex — multi-layered strata of sand, clay, and limestone that have been debated for decades. The commune produces wines of classical balance: aromatic, floral top-notes with firm but not aggressive cores.
Sant'Agata Marls (dominant)
Some Serravallian (east)
Top Crus
Cannubi ★ TOP CRU ›
Elevation
~230–300 m
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Complex multi-strata — layers of sand, clay, and calcareous marl. The geological debate over Cannubi's exact soil composition has raged for years. Unique within Barolo for its layered complexity.
Style
The most famous single vineyard name in all of Barolo — documented since the 1700s. Wines of classical balance: aromatic, perfumed, silky yet structured. Ages gracefully but more approachable young than Serralunga crus.
Producers
Bartolo Mascarello (source for blend) Luciano Sandrone Prunotto Paolo Scavino Marchesi di Barolo Francesco Rinaldi
Collector note: Undisputedly one of Italy's great "Grand Crus" with ancient documents marking its significance from the 1700s. Note the "Cannubi controversy" — several sub-zones carry the Cannubi name (Cannubi Boschis, Cannubi San Lorenzo, Cannubi Muscatel, Cannubi Valletta) but purists insist only the central hill is the "true" Cannubi.
Cannubi Boschis ★ TOP CRU ›
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Adjacent to Cannubi proper. Made famous by Luciano Sandrone — his first vintage (1978) from here launched the modernist movement. Slightly more structured than Cannubi proper.
Producers
Luciano Sandrone Francesco Rinaldi (as "Cannubi")
Collector note: Sandrone's Cannubi Boschis is one of Barolo's most iconic modern wines. The 2016 is a reference.
Sarmassa ★ TOP CRU ›
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Sant'Agata Fossili Marls — on the lower slopes of the La Morra hill, within Barolo commune.
Style
Powerful yet perfumed — a bridge between the aromatic character of La Morra and the structure of Barolo village. Among the most esteemed sites in the commune.
Producers
Marchesi di Barolo Brezza
Brunate (shared with La Morra) ★ TOP CRU ›
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Shared between Barolo and La Morra — one of the zone's most celebrated crus. Marries La Morra's seductive character with mineral austerity. Wines of extraordinary perfume and longevity.
Producers
Francesco Rinaldi Marchesi di Barolo Ceretto Vietti Roberto Voerzio
Liste ★ TOP CRU ›
Style
Well-regarded site, southeast of the village. Structured, full-bodied.
Producers
Borgogno (benchmark — one of Barolo's oldest producers, founded 1761)
Bricco delle Viole ★ TOP CRU ›
Elevation
~350–400 m (among the highest in the commune)
Exposure
South
Style
High altitude gives cooler microclimate — incredibly pure and airy Nebbiolo. Named for the wild violets that grow here. Floral, ethereal, crystalline.
Producers
G.D. Vajra (benchmark bottling)
Other Notable MGAs
Rue · San Lorenzo · Paiagallo · Fossati Notable ›
Rue — One of Bartolo Mascarello's sources for his iconic commune blend (0.5 ha)
San Lorenzo — Another Bartolo Mascarello source (0.25 ha); also Cannubi San Lorenzo sub-zone
Paiagallo — Source for Canonica's very sought-after Barolo (cult producer)
Fossati — Spills down from the La Morra peak into Barolo commune; shares the Rocche ridge's mineral character
La Volta · Terlo · Le Coste · Castellero Notable ›
La Volta — Western sector near Vergne hamlet; Cabutto estate
Terlo — Southern sector
Le Coste — Southern slopes
Castellero — East of the village, toward Monforte; Coste di Rose nearby
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Bartolo Mascarello Giuseppe Rinaldi Francesco Rinaldi Borgogno Giacomo Brezza
Terroir-First / In-Between
G.D. Vajra Marchesi di Barolo E. Pira & Figli (Chiara Boschis)
Modernist
Luciano Sandrone
Note: The village of Barolo is a stronghold of traditionalism. Bartolo Mascarello famously refused to bottle single-cru wines, blending Cannubi, Rue, Rocche dell'Annunziata and San Lorenzo into one iconic commune Barolo — and was fiercely anti-barrique ("No Barrique, No Berlusconi" was his motto). Giuseppe Rinaldi follows a similar philosophy. This contrasts with Sandrone, whose modernist Cannubi Boschis helped define the other side of the Barolo Wars.
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
Bartolo Mascarello — Barolo (commune blend)
No cru, no barrique. The purest expression of traditional Barolo. One of Italy's most iconic wines.
🍷
Giuseppe Rinaldi — Brunate / Tre Tine
Multi-cru blends of extraordinary depth. "Tre Tine" (three tridents) blends Ravera, Le Coste, and Cannubi San Lorenzo.
🍷
Sandrone — Cannubi Boschis
The modernist icon. First vintage 1978. Polished, concentrated, ageworthy.
🍷
G.D. Vajra — Bricco delle Viole
High-altitude purity. Floral, crystalline, unique within the commune.
🍷
Borgogno — Barolo Liste / Riserva
Founded 1761. Among the oldest producers in all of Barolo. Classic, structured.
🍷
E. Pira & Figli (Chiara Boschis) — Cannubi
One of Barolo's few female winemakers. Elegant, refined Cannubi.
📋Collector Strategy
For classic balance & perfume: Cannubi (multiple producers), Bricco delle Viole (Vajra)
For tradition & philosophy: Bartolo Mascarello (commune blend), Giuseppe Rinaldi (multi-cru blends)
For structure & aging: Brunate, Liste (Borgogno), Sarmassa
Key principle: Barolo village is where tradition and terroir philosophy matter most. The great producers here (Mascarello, Rinaldi) prove that blending across crus can rival any single-vineyard wine. Their allocations are tiny — buy when you see them.

Serralunga d'Alba

The Powerhouse — iron structure, vertical wines, legendary longevity
~310
Hectares
39
MGAs
220–420m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Serralunga d'Alba
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
Serralunga sits on a long, narrow north-south ridge on the eastern side of the Barolo zone — the most elongated commune in the appellation. Its vineyards extend from Fontanafredda's holdings in the north all the way to Francia and Arione at the southern tip bordering Monforte.
Serravallian dominance: Almost entirely on older Diano Sandstone — compact, calcareous marl that is less porous than the western communes' soils. This slows vine growth and produces wines of pronounced tannic spine, vertical structure, dark cherry, iron, and licorice. East-facing sites tend toward higher acidity and more aggressive tannins, while the prized south/southwest exposures deliver the commune's trademark power with ripe fruit. Masnaghetti calls it "a universe on its own."
Diano Sandstone (dominant)
Top Crus
Vigna Rionda ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
~10+ ha
Elevation
~270–350 m
Exposure
Predominantly south-facing
Soil
Compact calcareous marl — classic Serravallian formation. West of the village, just before reaching Monforte.
Style
Grand structure, sustained tannins, marked longevity. The ultimate expression of Serralungese power. Dark fruit, tar, licorice, iron — profoundly mineral. Needs decades.
Producers
Massolino (Riserva) Oddero Luigi Pira Ettore Germano Guido Porro
Collector note: Bruno Giacosa's legendary "Collina Rionda" bottlings (1967–1993) came from here. The vineyard was fragmented when the Canale family sold holdings — Massolino's Riserva is now the benchmark. Historically the most celebrated single vineyard in all of Serralunga.
Francia ★ TOP CRU ›
Elevation
~300–400 m
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Far southeast of the appellation — compact marl with limestone. Among the highest and most isolated sites.
Style
The source of Monfortino — arguably the greatest Italian wine ever made. Colossal structure, unrelenting tannin, extraordinary longevity measured in decades.
Producers
Giacomo Conterno (monopole since 1978)
Collector note: Fully owned by Giacomo Conterno since 1978. The source of both Barolo Francia (made every year) and Barolo Monfortino Riserva (made only in the best years — the most collected Barolo on earth). Also produces Barbera d'Alba Francia. Non-negotiable for any serious Barolo cellar.
Falletto ★ TOP CRU ›
Elevation
~280–380 m
Exposure
South-facing (the prized "Rocche" section) and east
Style
Grand, powerful, austere and regal. The south-facing "Rocche del Falletto" section yields the most concentrated wines. Bruno Giacosa's domain — his red-label Riservas from here are among the most collected Italian wines.
Producers
Bruno Giacosa
Collector note: Giacosa's red label = Riserva = top vintages only. "Falletto Vigna Le Rocche Riserva" is the pinnacle. White-label Falletto bottlings are also outstanding. After Giacosa's passing (2018), daughter Bruna continues. Prices have skyrocketed.
Lazzarito ★ TOP CRU ›
Area
Two sections (upper and lower)
Exposure
South to southwest — perfect exposition
Soil
Classic Serravallian marl. On the hill just after Prapò heading south toward the village.
Style
Tannic, structured, historically among the highest-priced grapes in Serralunga. Great depth and complexity.
Producers
Vietti Fontanafredda (Vigna La Delizia) Ettore Germano Guido Porro Schiavenza
Cerretta ★ TOP CRU ›
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Structured and age-worthy. Rising reputation — increasingly considered alongside Lazzarito. Located in the northern sector near Baudana.
Producers
Giacomo Conterno Giovanni Rosso Schiavenza Baudana (G.D. Vajra)
Collector note: Giacomo Conterno's Barolo Cerretta (from purchased vineyards) was their first bottling outside Francia — a seal of approval for this cru's quality. Giovanni Rosso is the other key reference.
Ornato ★ TOP CRU ›
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Full-bodied, rich, with the classic Serralunga iron backbone. Between Briccolina and Falletto.
Producers
Pio Cesare (flagship Barolo)
Other Notable MGAs
Prapò Notable ›
Area
~7 ha
Style
Perfect exposure, tannic and structured. Just north of Lazzarito.
Producers
Ceretto Schiavenza
Gabutti · Parafada · Margheria · Marenca Notable ›
A cluster of excellent sites on the hill just south of the village center. Historically among the highest-priced Nebbiolo in Serralunga.

Gabutti — Cappellano (famous for Chinato), Schiavenza
Parafada — Massolino (single-vineyard bottling)
Margheria — Massolino (benchmark bottling), extraordinarily beautiful position
Marenca — near Margheria, excellent southwest exposure
Baudana · Arione · Boscareto · Briccolina Notable ›
Baudana — Northern sector; Baudana estate (now G.D. Vajra)
Arione — Southernmost tip, bordering Monforte; Gigi Rosso, Roagna
Boscareto — South of the village, near Falletto
Briccolina — Between Ornato and Vigna Rionda
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Giacomo Conterno Bruno Giacosa Schiavenza Cappellano
Terroir-First / In-Between
Massolino Luigi Pira Ettore Germano Giovanni Rosso Vietti Guido Porro
Modernist / Large Estate
Fontanafredda Pio Cesare Ceretto
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
Giacomo Conterno — Barolo Monfortino Riserva
The most collected Barolo on earth. Made only in top years from Francia. Aged 7+ years in large cask.
🍷
Bruno Giacosa — Falletto Vigna Le Rocche Riserva (Red Label)
Red label = Riserva = top years. Austere, regal, legendary. Prices have skyrocketed post-2018.
🍷
Massolino — Vigna Rionda Riserva
The modern benchmark for Vigna Rionda. Grand structure, decades of life.
🍷
Giacomo Conterno — Barolo Francia
The "entry" Conterno. Made every year — still powerful and age-worthy.
🍷
Luigi Pira — Vigna Rionda
Newer entrant to Rionda (from 2014). Polished yet powerful.
🍷
Ettore Germano — Lazzarito / Cerretta
Captures Serralunga's precision and vibrancy. Rising star.
📋Collector Strategy
For ultimate power & longevity: Francia (Conterno), Vigna Rionda (Massolino Riserva), Falletto (Giacosa)
For structured depth: Lazzarito (Vietti, Germano), Cerretta (Conterno, Giovanni Rosso)
For value: Massolino Barolo DOCG (commune blend), Schiavenza Prapò, Guido Porro Lazzarito
Key principle: Serralunga rewards patience. Most cru wines need 10–15 years minimum. Buy in strong structured vintages (2010, 2013, 2016, 2019) and cellar long.

Monforte d'Alba

The Muscular Giant — Bussia, Ginestra, and broad-shouldered Barolo
~310
Hectares
32
MGAs
220–480m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Monforte d'Alba
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
Monforte d'Alba is one of the two great eastern communes (with Serralunga), sprawling across the southern portion of the appellation. It boasts some of Barolo's largest MGAs — Bussia alone is enormous and contains many sub-zones. The commune borders Barolo (west), Castiglione Falletto (north), Serralunga (east), and Novello (south).
Serravallian power: Predominantly older Diano Sandstone soils producing broad-shouldered, architectonic wines — black cherry, tar, balsam. Slow evolution, great longevity. Some western sites near Barolo village show Tortonian influence, adding fragrance. The wide variation in altitude (220–480m) and exposure creates significant internal diversity — Bussia alone spans multiple soil types and microclimates.
Diano Sandstone (dominant)
Some Tortonian (west)
Top Crus
Bussia ★ TOP CRU›
Area
Very large — one of Barolo's biggest MGAs, with multiple informal sub-zones
Exposure
Varied — south, southwest, and west sections
Style
The most famous Monforte cru — long reputed thanks to Prunotto and Aldo Conterno. Wines of depth, richness, and structure. Quality varies by sub-zone — Bussia Soprana (upper) tends more structured, Bussia Sottana (lower) more accessible.
Producers
Giacomo Fenocchio Prunotto Poderi Aldo Conterno Amalia (Vigna Fantini)
Collector note: Because Bussia is so large, quality varies more than most crus. Buy by producer and sub-zone rather than the Bussia name alone. Aldo Conterno's Gran Bussia and Romirasco bottlings are the benchmarks.
Ginestra ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South to southwest — warm, sheltered
Style
Powerful, warm, full-bodied — the quintessential Monforte muscle wine. Dark fruit, tar, spice. Among the most consistently excellent sites in the commune.
Producers
Domenico Clerico Conterno Fantino Elio Grasso Paolo Conterno
Mosconi ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South
Style
Gaining rapid recognition — muscular, concentrated, age-worthy. Southern sector, near the boundary with deeper Monforte.
Producers
Elio Grasso (Vigna Rüncot)
Collector note: Elio Grasso's Vigna Rüncot from Mosconi is one of Monforte's most celebrated wines — regularly scoring 95+.
Gramolere Notable›
Style
Central position between Bussia and Castelletto. Structured and mineral.
Producers
Fratelli Manzone
Castelletto Notable›
Style
East of Bussia, bordering Serralunga. More austere, Serralunga-like structure.
Producers
Monchiero Carbone
Ravera di Monforte · Bricco San Pietro · Perno Notable›
Ravera di Monforte — Southern sector; shares Ravera designation with Novello
Bricco San Pietro — The largest MGA in all of Barolo (380+ ha!). A catch-all designation; quality varies enormously
Perno — Hamlet; Giuseppe Mascarello's Santo Stefano di Perno bottling
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Giacomo Fenocchio Poderi Aldo Conterno
Terroir-First / In-Between
Elio Grasso Conterno Fantino Paolo Conterno Prunotto Fratelli Manzone
Modernist
Domenico Clerico
Note: Monforte is where the Conterno dynasty splits. Giacomo Conterno (Serralunga/Francia) and Aldo Conterno (Monforte/Bussia) were brothers who took different paths. Both estates are cornerstones of their respective communes.
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
Poderi Aldo Conterno — Gran Bussia / Romirasco
The great Bussia benchmarks. Gran Bussia is a multi-vineyard Riserva of legendary depth.
🍷
Elio Grasso — Vigna Rüncot (Mosconi)
Regularly 95+ points. Concentrated, age-worthy, modern classic.
🍷
Domenico Clerico — Ciabot Mentin (Ginestra)
Modernist icon from Ginestra. Powerful, polished, generous.
🍷
Conterno Fantino — Sorì Ginestra / Mosconi
Two top cru bottlings from one precise estate.
🍷
Giacomo Fenocchio — Bussia
Traditional Bussia at fair prices. Outstanding quality-to-value.
📋Collector Strategy
For power & depth: Bussia (Aldo Conterno), Ginestra (Clerico, Conterno Fantino)
For rising stars: Mosconi (Elio Grasso), Gramolere
For value: Giacomo Fenocchio Bussia — traditional quality at honest prices
Key principle: Monforte's size means internal variation is huge. Bussia alone could fill a cellar — but buy by producer and sub-zone. The commune rewards patience like Serralunga but with more mid-palate richness.

Verduno

The Smallest Commune — delicate wines, Monvigliero, and Pelaverga
~45
Hectares
8
MGAs
200–350m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Verduno
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
The smallest and northwesternmost of the main Barolo communes. Verduno sits at the top of the appellation's western ridge, producing Barolo's most delicate, high-toned, and early-drinking wines. Also the only commune famous for a non-Nebbiolo variety: Pelaverga Piccolo, a rare local grape making spicy, peppery reds.
Sandy Tortonian soils: Higher sand content than La Morra or Barolo village, giving wines of elegance, spice, and white-pepper lift — lighter in body but hauntingly aromatic. The best site, Monvigliero, has become one of Barolo's most sought-after crus, proving that delicacy and complexity are not opposites.
Sant'Agata Marls (sandy variant)
Top Crus
Monvigliero ★ TOP CRU›
Exposure
South to southwest
Soil
Sandy calcareous marl with good drainage. Steep, well-exposed amphitheater.
Style
Verduno's crown jewel and one of Barolo's most in-demand crus. Ethereal, perfumed, vibrant — white pepper, rose, wild herbs. Deceptive power beneath the elegance. Now cult-status.
Producers
G.B. Burlotto Fratelli Alessandria Comm. G.B. Burlotto
Collector note: Burlotto's Monvigliero has reached cult status — allocations are tiny and prices have soared. One of the most exciting crus in all of Barolo. Paolo Scavino also bottles a Monvigliero. A "must-buy when you see it" wine.
Massara Notable›
Style
Central Verduno site. Elegant, approachable. Source for both Barolo and Pelaverga.
Producers
Castello di Verduno
Neirane · Rocche dell'Olmo · Campasso · Breri Notable›
Neirane — Western slope; delicate
Rocche dell'Olmo — Source for Castello di Verduno's Pelaverga
Campasso — North of Monvigliero
Breri — Eastern sector
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
G.B. Burlotto Fratelli Alessandria
Terroir-First
Castello di Verduno
Don't miss: Verduno is the home of Pelaverga Piccolo — a rare, peppery, spicy red wine made nowhere else. Castello di Verduno and Burlotto both make excellent examples. It's not Barolo, but it's uniquely Verduno.
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
G.B. Burlotto — Monvigliero
Cult Barolo. Ethereal, perfumed, vibrant. Tiny allocation — buy on sight.
🍷
Fratelli Alessandria — Monvigliero
More structured take on Monvigliero. Excellent value vs. Burlotto.
🍷
Castello di Verduno — Massara
Classic Verduno elegance. Also makes benchmark Pelaverga.
📋Collector Strategy
Priority: Monvigliero from Burlotto or Fratelli Alessandria — one of Barolo's hottest crus
For discovery: Try Pelaverga Piccolo — the ultimate sommelier's wine and conversation starter
Key principle: Verduno wines drink earlier than most Barolo, but Monvigliero has proven it can age. Don't overlook this commune — it punches far above its size.

Novello

The Rising Star — cool altitude, Ravera, and Elvio Cogno
~78
Hectares
10
MGAs
300–480m
Elevation
MGA Vineyard Map — Novello
Source: Enogea / Alessandro Masnaghetti. Tap to zoom.
🪨Terroir
Novello extends south from Barolo village, occupying some of the highest-altitude vineyards in the appellation. Long overlooked, it has gained dramatic recognition thanks to the Ravera cru and the work of producers like Elvio Cogno and Principiano Ferdinando.
High altitude, mixed soils: Novello sits at the transition between Tortonian and Serravallian formations, with cooler temperatures from elevation (up to 480m). Wines show bright acidity, freshness, and mineral tension — structured but with a lively, vibrant character distinct from the warmth of Monforte or the mass of Serralunga. Climate change is making these higher, cooler sites increasingly valuable.
Tortonian (west)
Serravallian (east, toward Monforte)
Top Crus
Ravera ★ TOP CRU›
Area
Large — shared between Novello and Barolo commune
Elevation
~300–400 m
Exposure
South to southwest
Style
Now considered one of Barolo's very greatest crus — from relative obscurity to the top tier in a generation. Structured yet vibrant, with remarkable purity, mineral depth, and aging potential. The altitude gives freshness that warmer sites lack.
Producers
Elvio Cogno (Bricco Pernice from Ravera) Vietti Principiano Ferdinando
Collector note: Elvio Cogno and Vietti put Ravera on the map. Cogno's Bricco Pernice (from old vines in the heart of Ravera) is the benchmark — routinely 95+ points. Vietti's Ravera bottling is equally celebrated. This is a cru to buy now before prices climb further.
Cerviano-Merli Notable›
Style
Southern sector of Novello. Less well-known but gaining attention. Fresh, mineral.
Producers
Elvio Cogno
Bergera-Pezzole · Corini-Pallaretta · Ciocchini-Loschetto Notable›
Bergera-Pezzole — Northwest, borders Barolo commune
Corini-Pallaretta — Large central MGA, lower slopes
Ciocchini-Loschetto — Northern sector, between Bergera-Pezzole and Ravera
Sottocastello di Novello — Southern fringe, below the village
Producers
🏛Producer Style Map
Traditionalist
Principiano Ferdinando
Terroir-First
Elvio Cogno Vietti
Climate change angle: Novello's high altitude is becoming a major asset. As temperatures rise across Barolo, these cooler sites are producing wines with the freshness and acidity that lower communes increasingly struggle to maintain. Watch this commune closely.
🏆Benchmark Bottlings
🍷
Elvio Cogno — Ravera Bricco Pernice
Old vines, heart of Ravera. 95+ points routinely. The Novello benchmark.
🍷
Elvio Cogno — Cascina Nuova
The estate's "entry" Barolo from Ravera. Outstanding value gateway to the cru.
🍷
Vietti — Ravera
Vietti's Novello bottling — structured, mineral, age-worthy.
🍷
Principiano Ferdinando — Ravera
Traditional, long-macerated Ravera. Mineral, austere, rewarding patience.
📋Collector Strategy
Priority: Ravera — one of Barolo's fastest-rising crus. Cogno Bricco Pernice is the trophy; Cascina Nuova is the value play
Key principle: Novello is the climate-change commune. Buy Ravera now — it's already top-tier but prices still lag behind Cannubi, Vigna Rionda, and Francia. The altitude advantage will only grow.
📅Barolo Vintage Guide
General Barolo quality. Individual communes and producers vary. ★ = out of 5.
2023
★★★★ (tentative)
Peronospora pressure; early indications of balance and ripeness. Too early for firm judgment.
Drink: TBD
2022
★★★★
Hot, severe drought — yet surprisingly fresh, balanced, fragrant. Rounder than 2017, fuller than 2018. Some producers declassified crus due to low volume.
Drink: 2027–2037
2021
★★★★★
Widely hailed — structured with lush fruit, low pH, high acidity, restrained alcohol. Critics compare to 2016, 2010, 2001. Debated consistency.
Drink: 2028–2045+
2020
★★★★
Approachable, finer-framed, harmonious. Some prefer its balance to 2019. Commune-level wines drink earlier.
Drink: 2026–2038
2019
★★★★★
Near-perfect season. Vertical, tense, structured, vibrant. Rivals 2016 for aging potential.
Drink: 2028–2045+
2018
★★★½
Fresher, rainier. Best wines elegant but lighter overall. Charming and accessible.
Drink: now–2033
2017
★★★★
Hot, dry. Rich, ripe, softer-structured, earlier-drinking. Top sites in Castiglione and Serralunga proved their merit.
Drink: now–2035
2016
★★★★★
The modern benchmark. Balance, structure, age-worthiness. Castiglione was "a titan." Needs time.
Drink: 2026–2050+
2015
★★★★½
Warm. Ripe, approachable, balanced. Earlier-drinking than 2016 but excellent quality.
Drink: now–2038
2014
★★★
Cool, wet. Best wines elegant but lighter. Buy by producer, not vintage.
Drink: now–2030
2013
★★★★★
Cooler, classic. Purity, finesse, fine acidity, exceptional aging. Among the best since 2010.
Drink: now–2043
2012
★★★½
Atypical, uneven. Some elegant wines but less consistent across the board.
Drink: now–2028
2011
★★★★
Warm. Riper, more accessible, softer tannins. Enjoyable now.
Drink: now
2010
★★★★★
Legendary. Classic, structured, balanced, powerful. The reference vintage of the decade.
Drink: now–2040+
2009
★★★½
Warm, early-drinking. Pleasant but lacks the tension of 2010. Best wines have charm.
Drink: now
2008
★★★★
Classic, structured, underrated. Cool season gave fresh, racy wines with good acidity. Drinking beautifully now.
Drink: now–2032
2007
★★★★½
Warm, generous, ripe. Opulent wines with plush fruit. Early-drinking for the quality. One of the better warm years.
Drink: now–2030
2006
★★★★★
Outstanding classic vintage. Structured, aromatic, long-lived. Exceptional balance of power and finesse. Great aging.
Drink: now–2036
2005
★★★★
Cool year, elegant wines. Fine acidity and aromatics. Medium-weight but graceful. Drinking window is now.
Drink: now–2028
2004
★★★★★
Exceptional. Classic structured vintage — aromatic intensity, firm tannins, great longevity. Rivals 2001 in some cellars.
Drink: now–2035
2003
★★★
Extreme heat wave across Europe. Atypical, heavy, sometimes cooked. Best producers managed well. Drink soon.
Drink: now (fading)
2002
★★
Difficult. Rain, hail, rot. Many top producers declassified. Very few cru bottlings worth seeking.
Drink: past peak
2001
★★★★★
Legendary. One of the greatest Barolo vintages ever. Perfect balance, structure, complexity, longevity. Still developing. A desert-island vintage.
Drink: now–2040+
2000
★★★★
Very good. Warm, generous, ripe. Accessible and enjoyable. Overshadowed by 2001 but excellent in its own right.
Drink: now–2028
1999
★★★★½
Classic, structured. Excellent aging potential. Fine tannins, great aromatics. One of the decade's best — undervalued relative to 2001.
Drink: now–2032
1998
★★★★
Very good. Ripe, generous, approachable. Warm vintage with plush fruit. Most should be drinking now.
Drink: now
1997
★★★★½
Warm, early-ripening. Opulent, rich, concentrated. The modernists loved it. Great at its peak but drink up.
Drink: now
1996
★★★★★
Outstanding classic vintage. Structured, racy, beautifully aromatic. Serious longevity — still extraordinary from top producers. Giacosa's Riservas are monumental.
Drink: now–2035
1995
★★★½
Good but uneven. Decent structure, moderate depth. Overshadowed by 1996. Drink now.
Drink: now
1994
★★½
Difficult. Late-October rain spoiled many lots. Only the most rigorous producers succeeded.
Drink: past peak
1993
★★★½
Good. Medium-weight, elegant, aromatic. Not long-lived but charming. First vintage of Mascarello's Cà d'Morissio.
Drink: now
1992
★★
Weak. Rain and rot. Very few successful wines. Skip except from absolute top producers.
Drink: past peak
1991
★★½
Cool, lean. Some decent wines from sheltered sites but generally light and fading.
Drink: past peak
1990
★★★★★
Legendary. One of the all-time greats — warm, concentrated, powerful. Conterno Monfortino 1990 is iconic. Still evolving from the best producers.
Drink: now–2035+
💡Buying Strategy by Vintage
All-time legends (if you find them): 1990, 1996, 2001, 2004, 2006, 2010
Modern cellar priorities: 2013, 2016, 2019, 2021
Drink now / near-term: 2015, 2017, 2020, 2022
Buy by producer, not vintage: 2003, 2014, 2018 — seek out top names only
Avoid: 1992, 1994, 2002 — widely difficult years
📖Barolo Glossary
MGA (Menzione Geografica Aggiuntiva)
The official vineyard designation system introduced in 2010. Translates to "Additional Geographic Mention" — Barolo's equivalent of a cru. There are 181 across 11 communes. No formal quality hierarchy exists (unlike Burgundy's Premier/Grand Cru).
Nebbiolo
The sole grape permitted in Barolo. Named for "nebbia" (fog) — the autumn mists that blanket the Langhe. Late-ripening, thin-skinned, high in tannin and acid. The Michét and Lampia are the two main clonal selections.
Botti / Botte
Large Slavonian oak casks (typically 20–75 hL) used by traditionalists. They allow slow, micro-oxidative aging without imparting oak flavor. Plural: botti.
Barrique
Small French oak barrels (225 L) introduced in the 1980s by modernists like Elio Altare and Enrico Scavino. Impart more oak flavor and softer tannins. Now used in moderation by many producers.
Tortonian / Sant'Agata Fossili Marls
Younger geological formation (~7–11 million years) of blue-grey calcareous clay-marl. Found in western communes (La Morra, Barolo, Verduno). Produces more fragrant, elegant, earlier-maturing wines.
Serravallian / Diano Sandstone
Older formation (~12–14 million years) of compacted sandy-limestone. Found in eastern communes (Serralunga, Monforte). Produces more structured, powerful, austere, later-maturing wines. Historically called "Helvetian."
Sorì
Piedmontese dialect for a south-facing hillside with optimal sun exposure — the most prized vineyard orientation in the Langhe.
Bricco
The top or crest of a hill. Often the highest-elevation vineyard sites (e.g., Bricco Boschis, Bricco Rocche).
Riserva
Barolo aged a minimum of 62 months (vs. 38 for standard), including at least 18 in wood. Often from the best parcels in the best vintages.
Michét
A rare, low-yielding Nebbiolo clone prized for intensity, concentration, and aromatic complexity. Found notably in Monprivato (Giuseppe Mascarello).
Langhe
The hilly region in southern Piedmont where Barolo (and Barbaresco) are produced. Characterised by steep, vine-covered ridges and hazelnut groves.
DOCG
Denominazione di Origine Controllata e Garantita — Italy's highest wine classification. Barolo received DOCG status in 1980.
The Barolo Wars
The 1980s–2000s divide between traditionalists (long maceration, botti) and modernists (rotofermenters, barriques, shorter maceration). Largely resolved — most producers now occupy a middle ground.

Notes are saved to your browser's local storage. They persist across sessions — no internet needed.
